retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Types of Episiotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Median (midline) episiotomy Midline episiotomies are typically preferred in the United States. [7] The incision starts at the posterior fourchette and runs along the midline through the central tendon of the perineal body. The incision originates within 3 mm of the midline and is angled between 0 degrees and 25 degrees. The incision extends to about half the length of the perineum. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Types of Episiotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Modified-median episiotomy A variation of the median episiotomy. Two transverse incisions are added bilaterally, just above the anal sphincter, extending the median incision. The transverse cuts are perpendicular to the midline, with a total length of 2 to 5 cm. This approach aims to increase the vaginal outlet diameter by about 83% compared to a standard median episiotomy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Types of Episiotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Radical lateral (Schuchardt incision) A deep and fully extended incision into one vaginal sulcus, curving downward and laterally around the rectum. Often used in gynecologic procedures, eg, radical vaginal hysterectomy or trachelectomy, but may occasionally be used for complicated deliveries. The incision originates >10 mm from the midline and extends laterally around the rectum. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Types of Episiotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Mediolateral episiotomy The incision begins at the midline of the posterior fourchette but is directed laterally and downward at a minimum angle of 60 degrees. This method is designed to avoid the anal sphincter and is typically directed toward the ischial tuberosity. [4] The mediolateral episiotomy is more common in Europe and usually results in a 45-degree angle postdelivery. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Types of Episiotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral episiotomy This type begins further from the midline (>10 mm from the posterior fourchette) and is directed laterally. The cut is angled towards the ischial tuberosity, avoiding the midline and sphincter. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: An episiotomy, a surgical incision made at the end of the second stage of labor to widen the vaginal opening, helps facilitate delivery. [1] [2] Ideally, an episiotomy relieves pressure on the perineum during difficult deliveries, resulting in an easily repairable incision when compared to uncontrolled vaginal trauma. [3] The primary types of episiotomy are the midline (or median), which begins near the center of the perineum and extends straight downward, and the mediolateral, which starts similarly but angles 60 degrees to the side. [1] [2] Other less frequently used episiotomy incisions include the modified median, J-shaped, lateral, anterior, and radical. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy is a surgical incision made in the perineum as the patient is actively pushing to facilitate delivery using either scissors or a scalpel. [3] Various types of episiotomy have been developed, with the midline and mediolateral being the most commonly used.

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Types of Episiotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Each episiotomy technique is designed with specific considerations for the safety of both mother and child and to minimize complications, including anal sphincter injury (see Image . Episiotomy). [4] The different types of episiotomy incisions include:

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Types of Episiotomy
retrieved_docs.content: J-shaped episiotomy These incisions begin with a midline incision and curve laterally to avoid the anus. The initial incision is made along the midline, up to 2 to 5 cm from the anus. The cut is then curved towards the ischial tuberosity to divert the incision away from the anal sphincter. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for an episiotomy. Select the proper technique for an episiotomy. Implement appropriate postoperative care for a patient undergoing an episiotomy. Collaborate with an interprofessional team to provide care for patients who undergo an episiotomy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: An episiotomy is a surgical procedure performed during childbirth where an incision is made to widen the vaginal opening. There are two main types of episotomy: a midline (or median) episiotomy, which starts close to the midline of the perineum and extends downward in a straight line, and a mediolateral episiotomy, which begins similarly but extends laterally at a 60-degree angle. Episiotomy repairs are dictated by the severity of the perineal laceration following the infant's delivery; this procedure is generally treated like second-degree tears, with layered closure of the vaginal, muscular, and skin tissues. Continuous, non-locking sutures are often used to minimize pain and promote better healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Perineal Lacerations -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: An episiotomy is a surgical procedure performed at the bedside during the second stage of labor, which causes enlargement of the posterior vagina. [12] This is done just before the delivery to decrease maternal blood loss. An episiotomy may be indicated if there is a need for expedited delivery of the fetus, soft tissue dystocia, or a need to aid an operative vaginal delivery. [6] [8] [12] The 2 most common types of episiotomies are midline and mediolateral. [12] The midline episiotomy is the most commonly performed type in the United States and is associated with a higher frequency of severe perineal lacerations. [6] [8] [12] The mediolateral episiotomy is more difficult to repair and is associated with increased postpartum pain and blood loss. The routine use of episiotomy is not supported; therefore, the World Health Organization and ACOG recommend its restricted use. [6] [8] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Episiotomy ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Birth Delivery -- Technique or Treatment -- Cesarean Delivery
retrieved_docs.content: The cesarean delivery of multiple gestations involves the same steps as a singleton procedure. There is no preferred skin incision type for cesarean delivery of a multifetal gestation. For details regarding choices of skin incision, refer to StatPearls "Cesarean Delivery" for more information. [35] A skin incision is made, and dissection is performed through the subcutaneous layer and fascia, followed by separation of the rectus muscles and entry into the peritoneum. [35] An assessment of the degree of dextrorotation of the uterus is performed to evaluate the safest incision site for the hysterotomy. [36] The decision for classical versus low transverse incision is provider-dependent based on the accessibility of the lower uterine segment as well as the gestational age of the neonates. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Several countries have developed protocols regarding episiotomies to reduce the incidence of OASI. These initiatives, including the OASI Care Bundle in the United Kingdom, advocate for clinician training in perineal protection and the use of mediolateral episiotomies. Additionally, devices like EPISCISSORS-60 have been developed in the United Kingdom to help maintain the ideal 60-degree incision angle, reducing the risk of OASI. Studies have demonstrated these protocols have led to a significant decrease in OASI rates and have positively impacted clinician practices and patient experiences. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Access Incisions -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A small study compared the incisional hernia rate of midline vs transverse incisions and the results found a significantly lower rate of incisional hernias amongst the transverse incision group. A pooled data comparison study's results found a 10% risk of hernia for midline incisions vs 5% for paramedian incisions. Results from a meta-analysis found a 13% risk of midline hernia in the 2 years following surgery. Of those who underwent repair of their hernia, as many as 50% experienced a recurrent hernia. A recent study on the extraction site for minimally invasive colorectal surgery had results that also demonstrated a greater incisional hernia rate for a midline incision than umbilical, transverse, paramedian, or Pfannenstiel incisions. Results from a retrospective study on spinal surgery using a paramedian incision for anterior exposure demonstrated a 2.7% incisional hernia rate. Results from other studies have shown a lower incidence of incisional hernias using a paramedian compared with a midline incision, and several authors urge judicious use of the midline incision. [77] [78] [79] [80]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy and تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) are both classified under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد (obstetric therapeutic procedures). By recognizing these interventions as subcomponents of the broader category of obstetric therapeutic care, clinicians can better organize treatment options, understand their related indications, and apply appropriate management strategies within the field of obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1536
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy is identified as a specific therapeutic procedure within the domain of obstetric care, being categorized under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد. This surgical incision of the perineum, performed during the second stage of labor, is considered one of the specialized interventions used to facilitate vaginal delivery and protect surrounding tissues. Recognizing episiotomy as part of the broader set of obstetric therapeutic procedures underscores its role in managing childbirth complications and informs clinical decision‑making regarding when and how to employ this technique for optimal maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 167
retrieved_docs.content: is the parent of “therapeutic obstetric procedures” (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد); episiotomy is a preventive obstetric surgical procedure that belongs to the perineal procedure group بضع الارتفاق, is a child of “Operation to facilitate delivery,” and is the parent of “Repair of episiotomy” in the “Other procedures associated with delivery” category. Symphysiotomy is an inverse‑is‑a specialized perineal procedure within the same group. Anatomical sites such as the os pubis and the amnion are qualifiers for بضع الارتفاق and for the concept “abnormality,” while sweeping of the membrane is related to artificial rupture of membranes (AROM) with the amnion as the procedural site. Surgical Manipulation occupies a central node in obstetric care. It includes a broad spectrum of child procedures: application of therapeutic agent, combined obstetrical version (with internal and combined‑version‑without‑extraction subclasses, inverse of internal obstetrical version), delivery by double

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrical trachelotomy is presented as a component of routine obstetric care that includes antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care. In this context, obstetrical trachelotomy is regarded as synonymous with vaginal cesarean section, indicating that the procedure functions as a delivery method comparable to a cesarean performed through the vaginal route. Additionally, obstetrical trachelotomy is linked to the concept of a preventive incision, suggesting that the procedure may be employed prophylactically to reduce the risk of obstetric complications. Together, these relationships underscore the role of obstetrical trachelotomy within comprehensive maternal care, highlighting its integration with standard obstetric practices, its equivalence to a specific type of cesarean delivery, and its association with incision strategies aimed at preventing adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1666
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy, a preventive incision performed during vaginal delivery, is closely linked to its subsequent repair; the repair of episiotomy is classified as a specific type of episiotomy procedure and is grouped within the broader category of other procedures associated with delivery. Recognizing that preventive incision (episiotomy) and its repair are interrelated steps in managing perineal trauma helps clinicians organize delivery‑related interventions and provide appropriate postpartum care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2806
retrieved_docs.content: The operation to facilitate delivery, specifically the episiotomy, is classified under a broader parent category of obstetric surgical procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2255
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures associated with delivery are classified as subcategories within the broader group of other procedures to assist delivery and also fall under the specific category of repair of episiotomy, indicating that repair of episiotomy is considered one of the other procedures associated with delivery and that these interventions are part of the overall set of procedures used to assist childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2516
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery constitute a broad category of obstetric interventions that are employed to facilitate vaginal birth. Within this category, the specific complication فشل التوليد بالملقط (failure of forceps delivery) is identified as a child procedure, indicating that it is a particular adverse outcome associated with assisted delivery techniques. Episiotomy, a surgical incision made in the perineum to enlarge the vaginal opening, functions as a parent procedure within the same group, linking various delivery‑assisting methods and serving as a foundational technique for related interventions. Preventive incision, exemplified by episiotomy, is recognized as having a direct relationship to these obstetric procedures, underscoring the role of episiotomy as a preventive measure designed to reduce trauma during delivery and to support the successful execution of other assisted delivery methods. Together, these concepts illustrate how episiotomy and preventive incisions are integral components of the spectrum of procedures used to assist delivery, while also highlighting specific complications such as فشل التوليد بالملقط that can arise from assisted vaginal birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1790
retrieved_docs.content: Routine obstetric care, which includes comprehensive antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care, is broadly related to assisted delivery techniques such as forceps, vacuum extraction, and breech delivery, highlighting the integration of standard obstetric management with these specialized delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Repair of episiotomy is classified as one of the other procedures associated with delivery, indicating that it falls under the broader category of delivery‑related interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 551
retrieved_docs.content: of episiotomy, preventive incision). This integrated framework clarifies how each procedure is related, categorized, and applied within obstetric care, supporting comprehensive understanding and coordinated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1072
retrieved_docs.content: Labor and delivery (الولادة) is integrated with multiple data sources, including generic “Databases” and the “OBSTETRICAL GYNECOLOGICAL DATA BASE.” Routine obstetric care—ante‑partum monitoring, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy, forceps, vacuum or breech assistance), and post‑partum follow‑up—is directly linked to delivery modalities such as cesarean delivery (الولادة القيصرية), vacuum extraction (الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين), and the composite category “forceps; vacuum; and breech delivery.” Cesarean delivery is a method of الولادة that includes specific actions (Extraction), incision types (low transverse cesarean section), and scar classifications (CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN). Clinical indications and contraindications surround labor: premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) is a parent condition precipitating labor; abnormal labor (مخاض غير طبيعي) requires specialized management; placenta previa (بضع الارتفاق) dictates operative techniques such as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: Operation to facilitate delivery is a category that includes the episiotomy procedure. Other procedures associated with delivery encompass the repair of episiotomy. The repair of episiotomy is classified as a type of episiotomy, indicating that it is a specific intervention performed to close the incision made during the episiotomy. Understanding that episiotomy falls under operations to facilitate delivery and that its repair is both a distinct procedure and a subclass of episiotomy helps clinicians organize obstetric interventions and ensure appropriate postoperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2722
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive incision is a specific form of episiotomy that is performed prophylactically during childbirth to reduce the likelihood of severe perineal trauma; thus, it is directly related to the broader practice of episiotomy as a surgical technique used to enlarge the vaginal opening and protect maternal tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2294
retrieved_docs.content: Combined obstetrical version is a broad category of obstetric procedures that encompasses the specific technique known as internal and combined version without extraction. The internal and combined version without extraction is identified as a subtype of combined obstetrical version and is classified under the broader group of other delivery procedures, indicating its role as an alternative method used in the management of childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2377
retrieved_docs.content: The female perineal structure is the direct procedural site of a low forceps delivery with episiotomy.
